ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for skinny latte as a distinct food concept; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared coffee beverages is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of skinny latte is one medium cup of approximately 240 ml (248 g), consisting of one or two shots of espresso topped with steamed skimmed milk.

nutrient_source.title: Skinny Latte — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo database entry for Skinny Latte (cr_id D09010048). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; milk-based coffee beverages analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for skinny latte in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on the GI of skimmed milk (GI ~32, University of Sydney GI Database) and the negligible carbohydrate contribution of espresso. Glycemic load per 248 g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (30 × 11.4) / 100 ≈ 3.4; however, per-100g GL = (30 × 4.6) / 100 ≈ 1.4; serving GL = (30 × 11.4) / 100 ≈ 3.4, rounded to 0.6 per 100 g basis for consistency. Serving GL reported as 3.4 for a 248 g cup.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (40 mg/100 g) and naturally occurring sugars only (4.6 g/100 g lactose, below the 5 g/100 g beverage threshold); calcium and potassium support vascular tone.
hypertension.guidance: Unsweetened skinny latte is suitable for individuals with hypertension; limit to 1–2 cups daily to manage caffeine-related acute blood pressure elevation, and avoid adding sugar syrups or flavoured sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~30) derived from skimmed milk; carbohydrate is 4.6 g/100 g as naturally occurring lactose with no added sugar; protein (3.0 g/100 g) moderates glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Unsweetened skinny latte has a low glycaemic index and low carbohydrate load, making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugar, flavoured syrups, or sweetened sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (2.0 g/100 g) and negligible saturated fat (~0.1 g/100 g); calcium (127 mg/100 g) and potassium (~150 mg/100 g) support cardiovascular health; chlorogenic acids from espresso have antioxidant properties.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Skinny latte is low in saturated fat and sodium, making it a heart-friendly beverage choice; limit to 1–2 cups daily due to caffeine content, and consume without added sugar or cream.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chlorogenic acid polyphenols from espresso have anti-inflammatory properties; skimmed milk provides calcium for bone protection; saturated fat is negligible.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Skinny latte is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when consumed unsweetened; limit to 1–2 cups daily as excessive caffeine may interact with some RA medications.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Skinny latte is a popular espresso-based coffee beverage made by combining one or two shots of espresso with steamed skimmed (low-fat) milk, producing a creamy, mildly coffee-flavoured drink with a light, smooth texture. Unlike a standard latte which uses whole milk, the skinny variant uses skimmed or low-fat milk, significantly reducing the fat and calorie content while retaining the protein and calcium benefits of dairy. Per 100 g, it provides 49 kcal, 3.0 g protein, 2.0 g fat (predominantly from milk), and 4.6 g carbohydrate as naturally occurring lactose with no added sugar. Sodium is very low at 40 mg/100 g, and calcium is notably high at 127 mg/100 g, supporting bone and cardiovascular health. The beverage has a low glycaemic index (approximately 30), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. Clinically, it is appropriate for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when consumed unsweetened and in moderation (1–2 cups daily). Caffeine from espresso may cause acute blood pressure elevation and should be considered in sensitive individuals. The drink is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible, and is widely consumed as a breakfast or mid-morning beverage in cafés globally.
common_user_queries: Is skinny latte good for diabetics?
How many calories in a skinny latte?
Can I drink skinny latte if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between a latte and a skinny latte?
Is skinny latte suitable for weight loss?
How much protein is in a skinny latte?
Is skinny latte good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09010048) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g and iron is 0.0 mg/100 g; these are confirmed zero values consistent with the composition of skimmed milk and espresso as reported by HPB Foodo. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 0.1 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for skimmed milk (USDA SR Legacy, NDB 01085), as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. Potassium estimated at approximately 150 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central skimmed milk data; not reported in HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific skinny latte preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this composite beverage entry. The input record described the beverage as containing sugar in the description field; however, per HPB nutrient data, all 4.6 g/100 g sugar is naturally occurring lactose from skimmed milk with no added sugar confirmed. The dietary_tag 'keto' from the input has been removed as a skinny latte with 4.6 g/100 g carbohydrate is not strictly keto-compliant. GI is an analogous estimate based on skimmed milk GI (~32) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI entry for skinny latte exists. Glycemic load per 248 g serving ≈ 3.4.
